title: Questbound - a solo, rogue-lite card game (Review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vM5V9hoDKnQ

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews Questbound, a lightweight roguelike from the Game Crafter that sits somewhere between pure escape-room puzzle and economic management game. You're a plague-fighting professional running days (runs) through a procedurally-encountered map, managing supplies like a tightly-wound Euro while also picking fights with increasingly dangerous monsters. The skeleton here is familiar: defeat enemies by trading resources (supplies or lanterns) to add dice to your pool, complete quests for points, build permanent upgrades between runs like Rogue Legacy, and try to rack up a high score across six runs. Combat is refreshingly non-traditional—it's not about damage or hit points but about resource conversion and decision-making under pressure.

Daniel's main concern is variety. With only twelve base enemies, four characters, and a modest supply of market items and buildings, Questbound struggles to stay interesting across a full six-run campaign. Three runs feels like the sweet spot. However, the bones are solid, and with the promised expansions (which appear to be perpetually forthcoming), this could evolve into something genuinely compelling. The game shows real promise as a stripped-down, travel-friendly answer to deeper roguelikes—excellent for someone wanting roguelike mechanics in a pocket box.

----

Have you played games that improved dramatically with expansions? What would Questbound need to keep you engaged for a full campaign?
